STREET_DAYLIGHT.rationale: Zone focus / hyperfocal technique. f/8 at a fixed subject distance lets you shoot without raising camera, preserving candid quality.
STREET_DAYLIGHT.proTip: Pre-focus at 3 meters using the distance scale and everything from ~1.5m to infinity will be sharp at f/8.
STREET_DAYLIGHT.commonMistake: Raising the camera to your eye alerts subjects—spray-from-hip shots at pre-set focus distances give authentic results.

STREET_OVERCAST.rationale: Overcast streets give even, shadow-free light that 's ideal for candid faces. Open up from f/8 to maintain shutter speed.
STREET_OVERCAST.proTip: Rainy overcast streets give beautiful wet reflections—wet cobblestones and puddles add visual interest to street frames.
STREET_OVERCAST.commonMistake: Complaining about overcast instead of embracing the even light—it's actually ideal for street photography.

STREET_DUSK.rationale: Dusk brings the magical quality of mixed artificial and natural light. IBIS helps at 1/125s handheld.
STREET_DUSK.proTip: Dusk is the magic hour for street—neon signs, shop windows, and warm streetlights create atmospheric scenes.
STREET_DUSK.commonMistake: Stopping shooting when the sun sets—dusk light on streets is even better than golden hour for urban photography.

STREET_NIGHT.rationale: Night streets are low-contrast which taxes AF. Pre-focusing on a spot and waiting for subjects to enter is effective.
STREET_NIGHT.proTip: Find a pool of streetlight, focus there, and wait for interesting subjects to walk into the light.
STREET_NIGHT.commonMistake: Chasing subjects through dark shadows—find the light and let subjects come to you.

STREET_INDOOR.rationale: Markets, train stations, and shopping areas create dynamic motion with plenty of ambient light. 1/125s freezes most walking motion.
STREET_INDOOR.proTip: Slow shutter (1/30s) at train station platforms creates ghost-like motion blur of rushing commuters against sharp stationary elements.
STREET_INDOOR.commonMistake: Not using the architectural lines of stations and markets as compositional elements.

STREET_FAST_MOVEMENT.rationale: Freeze cyclists, runners, and fast-moving street subjects. 1/500 freezes most street-speed motion.
STREET_FAST_MOVEMENT.proTip: Position near a crosswalk or corner where subjects are predictable—use zone focus set to the anticipated position.
STREET_FAST_MOVEMENT.commonMistake: Chasing unpredictable subjects instead of positioning ahead of their likely path.

STREET_SILHOUETTE.rationale: Expose for a bright background (backlit window, arch, tunnel exit) and subjects become graphic black shapes.
STREET_SILHOUETTE.proTip: Tunnels, archways, doorways, and backlit intersections are perfect for street silhouettes—look for bright exit portals.
STREET_SILHOUETTE.commonMistake: Choosing colorful or busy backgrounds that compete with the silhouetted subject.

STREET_BLACK_WHITE.rationale: The classic street photography style of HCB and Doisneau. B&W removes distracting colors and focuses on geometry and human interest.
STREET_BLACK_WHITE.proTip: Look for strong geometric shadows that diagonal lines across the streets frame to create powerful compositions.
STREET_BLACK_WHITE.commonMistake: Shooting everything and converting to B&W—think in B&W and actively look for tonal contrast and geometry.

STREET_REFLECTIONS.rationale: Shop windows, puddles, and glass facades create layered worlds and surreal, double-image street frames.
STREET_REFLECTIONS.proTip: Shoot from an angle to include yourself or other reflected elements as part of the composition.
STREET_REFLECTIONS.commonMistake: Shooting straight on—the reflection becomes just a copy of what's visible. Use angles for surreal layering.

STREET_CLOSE_UP.rationale: Close-up details—hands, faces, textures, signs—create a complementary narrative alongside wider street frames.
STREET_CLOSE_UP.proTip: Include close-up detail shots in a photo story to create texture and intimacy alongside wider environmental shots.
STREET_CLOSE_UP.commonMistake: Only shooting wide establishing shots—close-ups are essential for building narratives in photo essays.

STREET_BACKLIT.rationale: Backlit street subjects with strong rim light. Expose for the lit edge and let shadows fall dark for drama.
STREET_BACKLIT.proTip: Morning and late afternoon sun low in the sky creates stunning backlit opportunities in cities with straight streets.
STREET_BACKLIT.commonMistake: Trying to expose for the backlit shadow side loses the dramatic rim light that makes the shot compelling.

STREET_CANDID.rationale: The heart of street photography. These settings are responsive enough to capture fleeting decisive moments.
STREET_CANDID.proTip: Develop your 'street awareness'—learn to read situations that are about to become interesting before they peak.
STREET_CANDID.commonMistake: Shooting too close, too aggressively, or with permission requests that destroy the candid quality.

STREET_RAINY_DAY.rationale: Rain creates wet reflections, umbrella colors, and a moody atmosphere that transforms ordinary scenes.
STREET_RAINY_DAY.proTip: Rain is a gift for street photographers—umbrellas frame subjects beautifully and reflective wet streets double the scene.
STREET_RAINY_DAY.commonMistake: Staying home when it rains—wet weather is some of the best street photography weather available.

STREET_LOW_ANGLE.rationale: Shooting from low angle (hip or ground level) creates dramatic perspective and unexpected viewpoints.
STREET_LOW_ANGLE.proTip: Photographing from ground level gives a child's-eye view of the world—compelling and unusual perspective.
STREET_LOW_ANGLE.commonMistake: Only shooting from eye level—changing perspective costs nothing and transforms ordinary scenes.

STREET_HIGH_CONTRAST.rationale: Harsh midday sun creates extreme shadows—usually bad for portraits but perfect for dramatic geometric street patterns.
STREET_HIGH_CONTRAST.proTip: Midday harsh light that most photographers avoid creates the strongest geometric shadow patterns on streets and buildings.
STREET_HIGH_CONTRAST.commonMistake: Avoiding harsh midday light—for abstract street patterns and sun/shadow geometry, it's unbeatable.
